title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 2]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   The text mentions the use of Arrhenius-type reactions: the general form is k = A * exp(-Ea/RT) where k is the rate constant, A is the pre-exponential factor, Ea is the activation energy, R is the ideal gas constant, and T is temperature.
*   The text discusses Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations, supplemented by the k-epsilon equations. Although the equations are not explicitly written, they are standard in turbulence modeling.
*   The text references the eddy dissipation concept (EDC) model.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 1:** Shows the enhanced FLOX burner with high power density operated with natural gas at 7 bars at a high adiabatic temperature of 1996K. The figure presents results from a numerical simulation and the flow field is indicated with streamlines. The top plot shows the molar fraction distribution of CO ranging from 0 to 14000 ppm (parts per million). The bottom plot shows the molar fraction distribution of NO ranging from 0 to 27 ppm. X-axis represents length and has units of mm.
CO molar fractions are much higher in the upstream region of the burner (approximately 14000 ppm). NO molar fractions are lower in the upstream (approximately 0 ppm) and highest as the flow extends downstream (approximately 27 ppm).
The trends indicate the production of NO from CO.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   No tables are present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   The text describes a schematic of the burner in Figures 2 and 3 (Not present on this page). The HiPerMix burner consists of a rectangular inlet channel (annular clearance) passing into a circular mixing pipe. Fuel is axially injected in line with the burner axis at the transition between the channel and the pipe. The channel height is smaller than the diameter of the circular mixing pipe.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 3]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

There are no explicit mathematical equations on this page.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 3: Computational result of flow and fuel concentration distribution in the HiPerMix burner:**
    *   **(a) Quadratic Inlet Channel:** Shows a circular cross-section of the mixing pipe. Colors represent fuel concentration, with red indicating 100% and blue indicating 0%. The fuel concentration is highly concentrated in the center of the circle. Secondary flow is almost zero.
    *   **(b) Rectangular Inlet Channel (aspect ratio height to width=1/2):** Shows a rectangular cross-section of the mixing pipe with fuel concentration color-coded. The fuel is more dispersed compared to the quadratic channel. A vector plot is overlaid, visualizing the secondary flow.
    *   **(c) Perspective Streamline Representation:** Shows fuel concentration in perspective view along the mixing pipe nozzle length (0-40 mm). The fuel inlet is visible. The fuel concentration is high at the inlet and then disperses as it travels down the mixing pipe. The color gradient represents fuel concentration (red to blue representing 100% to 0% fuel concentration).

The conclusion is that a rectangular inlet channel with a height-to-width ratio of 1/2 results in better fuel dispersion compared to a quadratic channel.

**3. Tables:**

There are no tables on this page.

**4. Diagrams or Schematics:**

*   **Figure 2: Schematic of the HiPerMix burner:**
    *   **(a) Perspective View:** A 3D illustration of the burner, showing the fuel injector, mixing pipe (cylindrical), annular clearance, and burner nozzle axis.
    *   **(b) Cross-Sectional Slice:** A cut-through view showing the fuel and air inlet arrangement.
    *   **(c) Longitudinal Slice:** A side view of the burner, showing the mixing pipe and air/fuel inlets. The burner exit leads to the combustion chamber entry.

The figures illustrate the basic components and geometry of the HiPerMix burner. It shows the air and fuel inlet configuration and the mixing pipe that promotes fuel-air mixing.

*   **Figure 4: Dome of a can combustor with 12 HiPerMix burner nozzles:**
    *   A top-down view of a can combustor dome with twelve HiPerMix burner nozzles arranged equidistantly around the combustor axis/center. The fuel injector and annular clearance around each nozzle are visible.

The figure shows how multiple HiPerMix burners are integrated into a can combustor. The burners are positioned to distribute fuel and air evenly within the combustion chamber.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 4]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   p = 7 bars (operating pressure)
    *   v_nozzle = 90 m/s (nozzle exit velocity)
    *   T = 673 K (air preheat temperature)
    *   lambda = 1.6 (equivalence ratio)
    *   T_adiabatic_flame = 1723 degrees Celsius (1996 K) (adiabatic flame temperature)
    *   PA = 13.3 MW/m^2 bar (area specific power density).
    *   H + O + M -> OH* + M (chemical reaction for OH* radicals formation)
    *   lambda = 312 +/- 20 nm (UV interference filter wavelength)
    *   f1 = -12.7 mm, f2 = 200 mm, f3 = 1000 mm (focal lengths of lenses in PIV setup)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   None found on this page.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   Table 1: Operating condition overview.
        *   Columns:  Parameter (description), 2A (Burner configuration), 2D (Burner configuration).
        *   Rows: Nozzle diameter (Ø_nozzle), pressure (p), Nozzle exit velocity (v_nozzle), air preheat temperature (T), Air mass flow (Q_air), Natural gas mass flow (Q_NG), Hydrogen content (f(H2)), Propane content (f(C3H8)), Thermal power (P), area specific power density (PA), volume specific power density (PV).
        *   Data: Contains the range or maximum values of each parameter (pressure, velocity, temperature, mass flow rates, fuel content, and thermal power per unit area and volume) for two burner configurations, 2A and 2D.
        *   Summary: The table summarizes the operating conditions for two different burner configurations, showing the range of pressures, velocities, temperatures, and fuel compositions used during the experiments. It highlights the differences between the enhanced FLOX burner versions 2A and 2D. Notably 2A has a higher power output for a wider range of operating conditions, where as 2D is more constrained.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   Figure 5: Combustor for high pressure test rig HBK-S.
        *   Description: Shows a photograph of the combustor setup, including the enhanced FLOX burner version 2A and hexagonal combustion chamber. The chamber has quartz glass walls facilitating optical access.
    *   Figure 6: Schematic of setup for PIV measurements.
        *   Description: Depicts the experimental setup for Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV). Shows a Nd:YAG laser emitting a beam that passes through a filter, convex lens and beam expansion optics to form a laser sheet. The sheet illuminates particles in the combustor. Light emitted from the particles is focused via a lens on a CCD camera. This allows for the measurement of particle velocities within a plane of the combustor via PIV.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 5]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   The text mentions using an interrogation window size of 64 x 64 pixels, corresponding to a spatial resolution of 7 x 7 mm².
    *   The text mentions that at position x = y = z = 0 mm, a coordinate system is defined at the intersection point of the burner's central axis and a plane 12 mm from the burner front plate.
    *   L95% = 4.2 mm (spatial resolution, CARS related)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 7:** This figure shows two arrangements of the burner and measurement plane for configurations of 0 degrees and 15 degrees. The diagrams illustrate cross-sections of the burner with the laser sheet positioned at these two angles.
        *   **Axes:** There are x, y, and z axes showing streamwise (horizontal), horizontal perpendicular to x, and vertical direction, respectively.
        *   **Trends:** The laser sheet position changes which changes where measurement occurs.
        *   **Key data points:** The coordinate system x=y=z=0.
        *   **Conclusion:** The burner and laser sheet can be configured with diffrent angles.
    *   **Figure 8:** The figure shows cross-sectional view of the combustion chamber with the enhanced FLOX burner. This figure illustrates where the CARS laser beams (pump and Stokes) are focused to create the probe volume. Notably, the generated CARS signal is collected with a second lens.
        *   **Axes:** No axes on graph, diagram is schematic.
        *   **Trends:** Schematic.
        *   **Key data points:** Laser focus spot.
        *   **Conclusion:** Shows how a CARS setup and beam direction will work.

3.  **Tables**:

    *   No tables are present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    * The information for figures 7 and 8 in section 2 contains these descriptions.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 6]
1.  **Equations:**

    *   𝐿95% = 4.2 mm (spatial resolution)
    *   𝑣nozzle = 90 m/s
    *   𝑣nozzle = 120 m/s
    *   𝑣nozzle = 70 m/s
    *   λ = 1.6
    *   λ = 2.0

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 9:** *Flame position and size for different fuels and air equivalence ratios*. This figure contains multiple subplots showing chemiluminescence images of the flame.
        *   **(a) CL 15°:** A schematic of the combustor geometry is displayed, where CL 15° notes the chemiluminescence image plane shown in (c) and (d).
        *   **(b) Nozzle positions:** shows the front view of the burner and the positions of the nozzles in the burner in a cross sectional grid.
        *   **(c) CL, NG:** Chemiluminescence image for natural gas (NG) with λ=1.6.  The x-axis is horizontal position in mm (0-120) and the z axis is the horizontal position in mm (0-20). Flame is most intense close to the burner.
        *   **(d) CL, NG + H2:** Chemiluminescence images for natural gas + 50% Hydrogen with λ=1.6 and λ=2.0. The x-axis is horizontal position in mm (0-120) and the z axis is the horizontal position in mm (0-20).  Flame length decreases with increased hydrogen and increased lambda.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   No tables present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Figure 9(a):** A schematic of the FLOX burner, indicating a 15-degree angle of some internal component.  It also indicates the size of the chemiluminescence image plane.
    *   **Figure 9(b):** Front view of the FLOX burner showing the projection/location of individual nozzles within it. The nozzles appear to be arranged in a circular pattern.

The page presents results from experiments investigating flame behavior in a FLOX burner. Chemiluminescence imaging is used to visualize the flame shape and position for different fuel compositions and equivalence ratios. The diagrams show the geometric configuration of the burner and the nozzle arrangement. The text describes the CARS technique used for temperature measurements and limitations related to turbulence and strong temperature gradients. The images visualize the change in flame length with the addition of hydrogen to the fuel.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 7]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   *p* = 7 bars (Pressure)
    *   *v_nozzle* = 90 m/s (Jet nozzle exit velocity)
    *   *T* = 673 K (Air preheat temperature)
    *   *T_ad* ≈ 2000 K (Adiabatic flame temperature)
    *   *u* = 0 (Axial velocity at stagnation points)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Fig 10: Pressure dynamics for different nozzle exit velocities**
        *   X-axis: Frequency, f [Hz]
        *   Y-axis: Amplitude, p [bar]. Plotted on a Log scale.
        *   Two plots are present. Plot (a) shows pressure dynamics at v_nozzle = 120 m/s and plot (b) shows pressure dynamics at v_nozzle = 70 m/s. 
        *   Plot (b), at the lower velocity of 70 m/s, has a clear peak at approximately 634 Hz and an amplitude of ~20.7 mbar.
    *   **Fig. 12(a): Average flow field (streamline plot) from PIV experiment**
        *   X-axis: x [mm]
        *   Y-axis: z [mm]
        *   Iso contour plot of velocity data overlaid with a streamline plot
        *   The PIV experiment shows a recirculation zone close to the air inlet.

    *   **Fig. 12(b): Average flow field (streamline plot) from numerical simulation:**
        *   X-axis: x [mm]
        *   Y-axis: z [mm]
        *   Iso contour plot of velocity data overlaid with a streamline plot
        *   The numerical simulation shows a recirculation zone close to the air inlet.

    *   **Fig. 13(a): Temperature and flow fields**
        *   X-axis: x [mm]
        *   Y-axis: Not explicit, but showing the shape of the burner.
        *   Color contour plot of the temperature distribution within the burner, overlaid with flow streamlines. Natural Gas Only.
        *   Temperature range presented is form 400K to 2200K.
    *   **Fig. 13(b): Temperature and flow fields**
        *   X-axis: x [mm]
        *   Y-axis: Not explicit, but showing the shape of the burner.
        *   Color contour plot of the temperature distribution within the burner, overlaid with flow streamlines. Natural Gas + 50% Hydrogen.
        *   Temperature range presented is form 400K to 2400K.

3.  **Tables:**  There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   **Fig. 11(a):**  Isometric diagram of combustor indicating the location of the PIV laser sheet and image plane. The angle is 15 degrees. The dimensions (size and position) of the area accessible to the measurements are indicated by a dashed rectangular frame. The combustor internal geometry is shown. A distance of 100mm from the inlet is labelled. 
    *   **Fig. 11(b):** Several instantaneous flow field visualization acquired via PIV.
        *   X-axis: x [mm]
        *   Y-axis: z [mm]
        *   Color bar: velocity [m/s]

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 8]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   Tmax = 2130 K (for natural gas)
*   Tmax = 2305 K (for hydrogen admixture)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Fig. 14: Temperature Measurements with CARS:**
    *   **Main Plot:** Shows temperature (K) on the y-axis versus axial distance (mm) on the x-axis.  Experimental CARS temperature measurements are shown, along with numerical simulation results. Three types of data are plotted: mean temperature (Tmean) indicated by square symbols, most probable temperature (Tmopro) indicated by circular symbols, and the interval containing 90% of all measured temperatures. A solid line shows results from numerical simulation.
    *   **Inlaid Frames (Histograms):** Three histograms showing the temperature distribution at axial distances 30mm, 110mm and 145mm. The y-axis is number of events and x-axis is temperature (K). The range of 90% of the temperature distribution is indicated for each axial postion.

    *   **Trends and Conclusions:** The overall temperature profile along the combustor axis, and assesses the accuracy of CARS measurements by comparing them to numerical simulations. Deviations are noted, especially near the stagnation point (x=30mm), and ahead of the exhaust. The histograms give insights into the temperature distribution, which is more expanded in the recirculation zone.

*   **Fig. 15: Exhaust Gas Analysis Data:**
    *   **(a) Plot for Natural Gas:** The y-axis represents CO, NO, and NOx concentration at 15% O2 dry exhaust (ppm).  The x-axis represents the air equivalence ratio (lambda). Experimental data for NO, CO, and NOx are plotted, alongside computational results. Computational results are for CO (squares) and NO/NOx (circles).
    *   **(b) Plot for Natural Gas + 50% Hydrogen:** Same axes as (a). Plots experimental data for CO, NO, and NOx, and computational predictions (squares for CO, circles for NO/NOx).

    *   **Trends and Conclusions:** These plots show pollutant emissions (CO, NO, NOx) as a function of the air equivalence ratio (lambda) for two different fuel mixtures: natural gas and natural gas + hydrogen.  Experimental data are compared to computational predictions. The plot shows trends of CO, NO, and NOx emissions as a function of equivalence ratio for two different fuel compositions, highlighting the impact of hydrogen addition on pollutant formation. For the natural gas case, numerical simulation predicts NO, while in the case of natural gas + 50% hydrogen, NO2 levels are overestimated.

3.  **Tables:**
    *   No tables are present on the page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**
    *   No diagrams or schematics of test rigs are present on this page. The text describes an exhaust gas analysis system of a high-pressure test rig connected to a suction probe behind the combustion chamber.

title: FLOX_Combustion_at_High_Power_Density_and_High_Flame_Tempera
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 9]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   p = 7 bars (Pressure)
    *   U nozzle = 133 m/s (Nozzle Exit Velocity)
    *   T = 673 K (Air Preheat Temperature)
    *   PA > 10 MW/m^2 bar (Thermal power density per area)
    *   Tad = 2000 K (Adiabatic flame temperatures)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 16: Measured NOx values for enhanced FLOX® burner, version 2D, for different fuel mixtures (natural gas, natural gas + propane, natural gas + hydrogen).**
        *   **Axes:**
            *   X-axis: Adiabatic flame temperature Tad [K] (ranging from 1400 K to 2200 K)
            *   Y-axis: NOx, dry, at 15% O2 [ppm] (ranging from 0 to 20 ppm)
        *   **Trends and Key Data Points:**
            *   The graph shows the relationship between adiabatic flame temperature and NOx emissions for different fuel mixtures.
            *   There are separate curves for:
                *   89% H2 + NG
                *   50% H2 + NG
                *   10% Propane + NG
                *   20% Propane + NG
            *   The NOx emissions generally increase with increasing adiabatic flame temperature.
            *   The hydrogen enriched fuels resulted in significantly higher NOx emissions compared to the propane enriched fuels at similar adiabatic flame temperature. A data point for natural gas (NG) is also shown, with lower NOx emissions compared to hydrogen.
        *   **Conclusions:**
            *   The fuel composition significantly affects the NOx emissions at a given adiabatic flame temperature. Hydrogen enrichment can lead to higher NOx emissions.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   No tables are present on this page.

4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**

    *   No diagrams or schematics are present on this page.
